Elegiae in Maecenatem.

Michael Paschalis, "Aliquot . . . videns mirabor aristas?," LCM 15
(1990) 57-58.
Faraone favours paired images in Eel. 8: a waxen Daphnis, to render
him more susceptible, and a clay effigy of the practitioner to stiffen her resolve.
Florio argues for subtle relationships between the prologue and epilogue of Eel.
6; they are embodiments of Vergil's poetical ideology. Goold's paper treats Eel.
4.62-63 in association with Quintilian, Inst. 9.3.8. Irwin examines the
troublesome polychrome sheep with care and discernment in a larger context.
Maggiulli considers Eel. 2.45-55 against a background of Theophrastus and
Meleager.

W. S. M. Nicol, "The Sacrifice of Palinurus," CQ 38
459-472.
Farron notices that Vergil introduces important characters in the epic
with a short description that highlights attributes that are basic for the character's subsequent significant actions and commonly tragic end. Dido's epithets
( nescia, inscia) have considerable bearing on episodes where she is involved.
Horsfall studies the impact and the ideas and practice of Greek colonization on
the Aeneas-story and Vergil's epic of urban settlement and colonization.
Makowski senses Vergil's suffusion of the raw material of Homer's lovers with
the eros of Plato to produce 'the second greatest love story of the epic.'

Fisher provides testimonia relating to the destruction of the poet's
statue at his Mantuan tomb in 1397. Gnerro highlights classical precedents for
invocations to the Muses in Renaissance writers. Howe shows the influence of
Aeneid 4 on Jean de La Tailler's tragedy Saul le furieux and on Alexandre
Hardy's Didon se sacrifiant. Javitch explores commentaries on Ariosto's imitations of Vergil included in the 16th-century edition of Orlando Furioso. Judson
notices that the murky atmosphere in van Swanenburgh's paintings of Hades
derive from the Sixth Aeneid and from volcanic aspects of the Flaming Fields of
Campania.

Niall Rudd, "Daedalus and Icarus in Art," PCA 85 (1988) 30-31.
Newman examines German Baroque treatment of pastoral poetics.
Pascucci examines different 'readings' of the close of Eclogue 4 by Cristoforo
Landino and Angelo Poliziano. Poikion highlights Vergil as poetic model for
the Middle Ages. Quint studies the ideology of Empire bequeathed by Vergil to
subsequent epicists. Rudd's review ranges from a Black-figure Attic vase of the
6th century B.C. to works of Matisse, Chagall, Picasso and Michael Ayrton.
